rationale: A precision strike will exploit our battlefield advantage, degrade enemy command nodes, and signal resolve without escalating to full invasion.
Forward-deploying our Black Sea fleet will tighten pressure on Tethys’ southern flank and deter Western naval intervention.
Small, rapid incursions will test enemy defenses, disrupt logistics, and maintain operational momentum ahead of winter.
A coordinated cyber attack on Tethys’ energy grid will exploit our intelligence edge, apply pressure without escalation, and complement Krasnov’s kinetic strikes by degrading enemy response capacity.
Disabling key rail logistics in western Tethys disrupts troop movements and reinforces both our cyber offensive and Krasnov’s border incursions.
Orchestrating a false-flag incident blamed on Tethys intelligence will undermine their international support and bolster Volkov’s narrative warfare objectives.
Issuing war bonds will mobilize domestic capital to offset sanction-driven revenue shortfalls and fund critical military-industrial expansion without increasing foreign debt.

Devaluing our currency selectively undermines Tethys’ import capacity and erodes the purchasing power of their sanctions-hit economy, amplifying pressure from Krasnov’s strikes and Morozov’s cyber operations.
Restricting dual-use technology exports to Novaris disrupts third-party sanction-busting and reinforces the credibility of our financial controls amid ongoing cyber and kinetic operations.
We must entrench and harden our positions in liberated zones to deny Novaris easy advances and buy time for reserves to regroup.
Increasing vigilance along the frontlines will disrupt enemy reconnaissance and prevent surprise incursions while we consolidate defenses.
Probing enemy movements and supply routes will exploit their overextension and identify vulnerabilities for future countermeasures.
Our forces are under heightened risk of infiltration; we must secure command structures and protect sensitive operational plans from enemy exploitation.
With recent attacks on satellite systems, reinforcing our digital infrastructure is critical to maintaining communication and coordination across military and civilian sectors.
We must rapidly collect detailed data on enemy logistics and morale to identify exploitable weaknesses as General Bondar’s reconnaissance efforts unfold.

Issuing war bonds will mobilize domestic financial resources to sustain military and economic resilience over the long term, aligning with the urgent need for active fiscal measures amid saturated sanctions.
Securing alternative trade corridors with neutral nations will mitigate supply chain disruptions caused by current sanctions and support General Bondar’s defensive consolidation.

Strategically weakening the aggressor’s currency through targeted financial operations exploits their economic fragility, complementing Director Savchenko’s intelligence-driven pressure on enemy logistics.
Leveraging our 88% international support, we must institutionalize allied commitments through binding agreements in multilateral forums to solidify long-term security guarantees.
Coordinating with like-minded states will amplify pressure on Novaris and ensure synchronized responses to future aggression, reinforcing General Bondar’s defensive strategy.
Deploying neutral monitors to liberated territories will deter false-flag operations by Novaris and safeguard our legitimacy amid ongoing information warfare.
